title: Sampling Distribution of the Sample Proportion

epigraph.text: So the last will be first, and the first last.
epigraph.quote: Every proportion is a probability in disguise.

content.text:
For categorical data, we estimate population proportions using sample 
proportions. The sampling distribution of $\phat$ follows from the CLT.

content.title: Sample Proportion
content.content:
For a sample of $n$ independent observations, where each has probability 
$p$ of "success," the **sample proportion** is:
\[
 \phat = \frac{X}{n}
\]
where $X$ is the number of successes (so $X \sim \Binom(n, p)$).

content.title: Sampling Distribution of $\phat$
content.content:
If $\phat$ is the sample proportion from $n$ independent trials with 
success probability $p$:
\[
 \E[\phat] = p \qquad \text{and} \qquad \SE(\phat) = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Sampling Distribution of Proportion
content.problem:
A candidate has support from 45\% of voters. In a poll of 400 voters, 
what is the probability the sample shows more than 50\% support?
